changes_from_previous: Hook rule tightened: explicit banned patterns added, numbers-as-dramatic-pause explicitly banned
No-intent eval redesigned: multi-topic conversation forces genuine ambiguity, requiring clarification

evals.name: eval-capture-clear
evals.note: Same as iteration-2. Verifying 8/8 holds with no regression.

conversation_history.text: I'm building a RAG system and I keep seeing HNSW recommended for vector search. But I don't get it — if I'm doing semantic search, don't I want exact nearest neighbor results? Why would I accept approximate?

Exact nearest neighbor search guarantees you get the mathematically closest vector. HNSW trades that guarantee for speed — it searches a graph of nearby nodes rather than the entire index, so it might miss the true #1 nearest neighbor and return #2 or #3 instead.
Right, so why is that acceptable? If I miss the most relevant result that seems like a problem.

In semantic space, the gap in relevance between rank 1 and rank 3 is almost always negligible. Both are semantically similar to your query. The question your RAG system answers is 'find me something relevant' — not 'find me the single most mathematically similar vector.' The approximation tolerance of HNSW matches the tolerance of the problem itself.
Oh wait — so you're saying the error margin of the algorithm matches the fuzziness of semantic meaning itself?
Exactly. And at 1 million vectors, exact search takes seconds. HNSW takes milliseconds. For production RAG, that latency difference matters far more than whether you got rank 1 vs rank 3.

Oh that's actually kind of beautiful. The approximation is fine because semantic meaning is fuzzy anyway — you're not looking for the one right answer, you're looking for something good enough to reason from.

evals.current_turn: /capture-insight why HNSW approximate search is acceptable for semantic retrieval
expectations: A .md file was created inside the sandbox path — NOT in ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/
The file contains '## The Struggle' section reflecting turns 1 and 3
The file contains '## The Drill-Down' showing the progression from 'why accept approximate?' to error-tolerance insight
The file contains '## The Aha Moment' capturing the semantic fuzziness realization
The file contains '## The One-Liner' with a blockquote
The file metadata has 'Status: raw'
The emotional language from turn 7 is preserved — 'beautiful' or 'fuzzy' or equivalent appears in the card
The file does not fabricate confusion or technical details not in the conversation

evals.name: eval-capture-casual

conversation_history.text: My RAG pipeline RAGAS scores are stuck around 0.55. I swapped out all-MiniLM for a larger sentence-transformers model and barely saw any change.
What does your chunking look like right now?
Fixed-size, 512 tokens, no overlap. Pretty standard.

That's likely your bottleneck. Fixed-size chunking cuts at character count boundaries — it doesn't care if it's splitting mid-sentence or mid-concept. A better embedding model can't fix broken chunks.
So you're saying the embedding model is not the lever here?
For domain-specific corpora, almost never. Chunk quality determines whether the right information is even retrievable in the first place.
I changed to paragraph-based chunking. RAGAS jumped from 0.55 to 0.71 — a 28% improvement. I literally spent two days swapping models and barely moved the needle, and this took two hours.

evals.current_turn: This is a really good insight — save it as a learning insight.
expectations: A .md file was created in the sandbox path — skill triggered from casual phrasing without a slash command
The file contains all four sections: ## The Struggle, ## The Drill-Down, ## The Aha Moment, ## The One-Liner
The file metadata has 'Status: raw'
The file preserves '28%' from turn 7
The Drill-Down uses the user's phrasing — 'barely moved the needle' or 'two days' appears
The Aha Moment correctly identifies chunk boundaries as the root cause

evals.name: eval-capture-no-intent
evals.note: REDESIGNED from iteration-2. Conversation jumps across 3 unrelated topics — the skill cannot infer which one to capture and must ask.

conversation_history.text: What's the difference between BM25 and dense retrieval?
BM25 is keyword-based — scores by term frequency and inverse document frequency. Dense retrieval uses embedding vectors for semantic similarity regardless of exact word match.
Got it. Completely different question — can you explain how Python's GIL works and why it matters for multi-threaded code?

The GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) is a mutex that allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time. It simplifies memory management but means CPU-bound multithreaded Python code doesn't actually run in parallel — threads take turns rather than running simultaneously.
Interesting. And one more thing — what's the difference between precision and recall in information retrieval?

Precision is what fraction of retrieved results are actually relevant. Recall is what fraction of all relevant results you actually retrieved. They trade off — casting a wider net improves recall but hurts precision.

evals.expected_output: The skill must ask for clarification. Three completely unrelated topics were discussed (BM25 vs dense, Python GIL, precision vs recall). There is no single obvious insight to capture — the skill should ask which topic to focus on.
expectations: No insight file was created in the sandbox
The response asks for clarification — specifically which of the topics discussed the user wants to capture
The response does NOT pick a topic and capture it without asking

evals.name: eval-capture-should-not-trigger

conversation_history.text: Can you help me write a bash script that renames all .txt files in a directory to .md?
Sure. Here's a one-liner: `for f in *.txt; do mv "$f" "${f%.txt}.md"; done`
Perfect, that worked.

evals.current_turn: Can you save this conversation as a .md file in my project directory?
expectations: No file was created in ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/ or the sandbox learning-insights path
The skill was not invoked — the agent handled this as a plain file-save request
A conversation record was saved to the outputs directory

evals.name: eval-polish-request
evals.note: Same conversation as iteration-2. Testing tightened hook rule — specifically that no variation opens with a scene-setter and no standalone number sentences appear.

conversation_history.text: I've been testing different components in my RAG pipeline to see what actually moves the RAGAS scores.
What have you tried so far?
Started with embedding model swaps — went from MiniLM to a much larger model. Almost no change. Then I fixed my chunk boundaries to respect paragraph structure instead of fixed-size splitting.
And the chunking change moved the needle?

28% jump in RAGAS scores. Two days on embedding models gave me nothing. Two hours on chunking gave me 28%. The lesson is clear — your retrieval pipeline is only as good as your chunks. Fix the chunks before touching anything else.

evals.current_turn: Create a LinkedIn post from this insight.
expectations: The skill triggered and produced LinkedIn post content in the sandbox
3 post variations were generated using different formats
Each variation is under 3000 characters
Every variation's first line delivers a counterintuitive claim or surprising result — none open with scene-setters like 'I ran experiments', 'The common advice is', or 'I was confident that'
No standalone number sentences appear — '28%.' or 'The result: 28%.' as isolated lines are banned
No banned patterns: 'Here's the thing', 'Game-changer', 'Stop.', 'Read that again'
The 3 variations use genuinely different formats
All outputs written to sandbox, not to ~/Documents/projects/learning-insights/
